results.possibleIdentity: Tony (Anthony) Martin (1937–2021) was an American multimedia artist, painter, and pioneer of light art and interactive installations. Active from the early 1960s, he was a key figure in the integration of technology and visual arts. Martin served as the first visual director of the San Francisco Tape Music Center, where he collaborated with composers like Morton Subotnick and Pauline Oliveros and created psychedelic light shows for iconic bands such as the Grateful Dead and Jefferson Airplane at venues like the Fillmore West. In the late 1960s, he moved to New York City and joined NYU's Intermedia Program. He was significantly involved with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), most notably contributing to the 1966 '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' performances by designing the lighting for David Tudor's 'Bandoneon! (a combine).' He also played a critical role in the design of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, where he developed a laser deflection system and a programmable lighting system. His solo works, such as 'The Well' (1967) and 'The Game Room' (1968) at the Howard Wise Gallery, were groundbreaking for their use of sensors and photoelectric cells to create interactive environments that responded to viewer movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Fiala (potentially Ralph Robert Fiala) is an artist, choreographer, and dancer active in the late 1960s and 1970s, known for his work in experimental and multi-media performance. He served as the Assistant Artistic Director and a choreographer for the Multigravitational Experiment Group (also known as the Multigravitational Group or Aerodance), a New York-based collective founded by Stephanie Evanitsky. The group was recognized for developing 'aero-dance,' a form of performance where dancers moved while suspended from a large metal scaffolding system using ropes, slings, and plastic tubes. Fiala's work with the group included choreographing and composing scores for pieces such as 'Splat' and 'Alien Connection.' The group was a resident of the Space for Innovative Development (SID) in New York and performed at prominent venues including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) Summergarden (1974), the Grand Palais in Paris (1972), and the Fylkingen Theater in Stockholm. Fiala's artistic activities align with the 'Art and Technology' movement of the era; he was a recipient of a Canada Council bursary in 1969-1970 while based in Montreal, appearing in reports alongside organizations like Intermedia and Intersystems that were central to Canadian experimental and technological art. His name has also appeared in later contexts as a collaborator with dance and voice artists such as Susanna Hood.

results.possibleIdentity: Lorna Shanks was a prominent professional in the graphic design and art administration fields during the 1970s and early 1980s. She is best known for her significant roles at the International Typeface Corporation (ITC) and its influential typography journal, U&lc (Upper and Lower Case). At U&lc, she served in several key capacities, including Advertising Manager (beginning in 1979), Contributing Editor (1981), and Research Director (1982). Her work often focused on the intersection of design and emerging technologies, such as her involvement in the "Vision '77" and "Vision '80s" reports on new typographic technologies. In addition to her editorial work, she was a writer and designer, credited with designing and writing articles for U&lc as early as 1977. Shanks was also active in the public arts sector, appearing in the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) Annual Reports for 1974 and 1977, where she was associated with the Architecture + Environmental Arts program. In 1975, she served as a panelist at the Second Federal Design Assembly on the topic "Does It Have to Be Printed?". Her professional activities align closely with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as her career was dedicated to the collaboration between artists and technical experts during the technological shifts in the graphic arts industry.

results.possibleIdentity: Lucio Pozzi (born November 29, 1935) is an Italian-born American multidisciplinary artist who became a significant figure in the New York avant-garde scene starting in the 1960s. After studying architecture in Rome, he moved to the United States in 1962 as a guest of the Harvard International Summer Seminar and settled in New York City. Throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he was deeply involved in the experimental art movements of the time, including conceptual art, performance, and video art. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s, appearing in their records as a participant in their 'matchings' program that paired artists with engineers. Pozzi is renowned for his 'Inventory Game,' an improvisational method involving a lexicon of materials and techniques. He was a founding editor of the influential journal 'October' (contributing its initial design and name) and later founded 'New Observations' magazine. His extensive career includes solo exhibitions at the Museum of Modern Art (1978 Projects series), participation in Documenta 6 (1977), and representation in the American Pavilion at the 1980 Venice Biennale. His work is held in major collections such as the Museum of Modern Art, the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Museum of Contemporary Art, Chicago. As of 2025, he remains active, with major career retrospectives held at institutions like Magazzino Italian Art.

results.possibleIdentity: William Soghor is an artist and entrepreneur active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his participation in the group exhibition 'Soft as Art,' held from March 20 to May 6, 1973, at the New York Cultural Center (in association with Fairleigh Dickinson University). This exhibition, curated by Mario Amaya, explored the use of soft materials and included prominent artists such as Robert Rauschenberg, Robert Morris, Claes Oldenburg, Robert Mapplethorpe, and Richard Serra. In addition to his artistic practice, he founded The William Soghor Company, which produced specialized artwork inventory systems, including file cards designed to hold 35mm slides, which were utilized by artists for archiving their portfolios. He is associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) network, appearing in bibliographic and historical records alongside the organization. He is married to the portrait painter Phyllis Herfield and has family ties to both New York and Los Angeles.
